nativeRange.habitat: The Two-Spot Octopus is found along coastal rocky reefs, debris-strewn shorelines, and intertidal flats from mid- and southern California south to Pacific Baja California. In the wild it favors crevices, rock piles, and shell debris as shelter, and is encountered from the intertidal zone down to about 20 meters.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: The Two-Spot Octopus is an active predator that feeds exclusively on animal prey. In the wild it has been documented hunting crabs, shrimp, bivalves, snails, and small fish. In captivity it generally accepts thawed frozen shrimp, scallops, and clam meat, as well as live crustaceans such as fiddler crabs, shore shrimp, and hermit crabs. Juveniles typically start on small prey — amphipods and mysid shrimp — before progressing to larger items, and hatchlings under about three months old are usually fed multiple times per day. Freshwater feeder fish such as goldfish are nutritionally poor and have been linked to health problems in captive cephalopods; they are best avoided.

goodWith: kept strictly as a species-only tank
avoidWith: corals (may be uprooted or harmed by jetting)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by velodrome (iNaturalist), 2048x1542. Sharp, vibrant face-on tidepool portrait of a live California two-spot octopus with green-tan camouflage, orange sucker rims and clear eyes; the most striking in-situ shot.
Photo by Jerry Kirkhart, 4288x2848. Sharp, high-res full-body view of the whole animal in eelgrass showing complete arm and mantle anatomy; clean reference shot.

Photo by Kate Hildenbrand, 1500x1124. Colorful in-situ reef scene showing the octopus camouflaged among red strawberry anemones and urchins; illustrates natural coastal habitat, though the animal is partly cryptic.

summary: The Two-Spot Octopus (Octopus bimaculoides) is widely considered the most popular pet octopus in the hobby, valued for its diurnal activity, moderate size, and coastal California origin. It requires a coldwater chiller, a fully escape-proof tank, and a species-only setup.
careNotes: The Two-Spot Octopus is generally considered the most beginner-accessible pet octopus, but it remains firmly a specialist-only animal in the home aquarium. Its coastal-California origin means it requires cold water — most sources advise keeping it between 16 and 22 C, which almost always means a dedicated aquarium chiller in a heated home. Containment is the other defining challenge: octopuses can squeeze through any gap wider than their beak, and the Two-Spot has been documented escaping from unsealed tanks. A tightly-fitting lid secured with weights or latches, sealed overflow weirs, and screened powerhead intakes are all essential. The species is also highly intelligent and benefits from ongoing environmental enrichment — caves, PVC pipe dens, and novel objects introduced regularly. The tank must be species-only: any fish, shrimp, crab, or snail will eventually be eaten, and two octopuses kept together will fight. Lifespan in captivity is short, typically 1 to 2 years, and females normally die shortly after their eggs hatch — a natural end-of-life process tied to reproduction. Like all molluscs, the Two-Spot Octopus is highly sensitive to copper and should never share a system with copper-based medications. Captive-bred individuals are available from a small number of specialist breeders, but most specimens in the trade are still wild-collected from the southern California coast. The species was the first octopus to have its genome fully sequenced (in 2015) and is widely used as a research animal in cephalopod neuroscience.
breedingNotes: Females lay strings of comparatively large eggs — around 1 cm each — attached to a hard surface inside a den, and brood them without feeding until they hatch. Unlike many marine invertebrates, the hatchlings emerge as fully-formed bottom-dwelling miniatures of the adult rather than passing through a free-swimming planktonic larval stage; this is one of the main reasons the Two-Spot Octopus is among the few octopuses that can realistically be reared in captivity. Even so, hatchling survival rates are low, and the young require live amphipods and mysid shrimp in the early weeks. Females die shortly after their eggs hatch — this is a natural, genetically programmed end-of-life event tied to reproduction, not illness. Spontaneous infertile egg-laying has also been reported in unmated captive females.

primary.name: TONMO - Octopus bimaculoides Care Sheet
primary.notes: Substituted as primary after both originally-supplied sources (LiveAquaria, TFH product page) returned 404. TONMO is the long-running cephalopod-keeping community and the most-cited care reference for this species.

additional.name: TFH Magazine - Choosing and Keeping a Pet Octopus
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (75 US gal / 284 L recommended vs TONMO 50 US gal / 189 L floor), coldwater requirement (<21 C), and species-only / no-fish guidance. Averaged tank size upward to 300 L per the playbook bias-toward-larger rule for intelligent escape-prone animals.

additional.name: Wikipedia - California two-spot octopus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Octopodidae, Octopoda), native range (southern California to western Baja California), depth (intertidal to ~20 m), adult size (17.5 cm mantle / 58 cm arms), preferred temperature 18-22 C with tolerance 15-26 C, lifespan 1-1.5 yr wild / up to 2 yr captive, and benthic-hatchling life history that makes the species one of the few captive-rearable octopuses.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Advanced setup required: coldwater chiller, fully escape-proof lid with locking weights, sealed overflows and powerhead intakes, strictly species-only tank, very short captive lifespan (~1-2 years), and highly intelligent animal that needs ongoing environmental enrichment.
